1.title: 1st person
1.html: Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

2.title: 2nd person
2.html: Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3.title: 3rd person
3.html: Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There's no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

noun.title: Noun
noun.html: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. In Latin, nouns inflect for (six forms — nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, ablative, vocative) and (singular and plural). Every noun belongs to one of five and carries a fixed (masculine, feminine, or neuter), shown in the dictionary entry.

verb.title: Verb
verb.html: A word that names an action or a state. A Latin verb inflects for , , , , and . One verb has roughly 150 distinct forms; the four listed in the dictionary entry are enough to generate them all.

adj.title: Adjective
adj.html: A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

adjective.title: Adjective
adjective.html: A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

pron.title: Pronoun
pron.html: A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

pronoun.title: Pronoun
pronoun.html: A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

prep.title: Preposition
prep.html: An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

preposition.title: Preposition
preposition.html: An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

conj.title: Conjunction
conj.html: An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

conjunction.title: Conjunction
conjunction.html: An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

adv.title: Adverb
adv.html: An indeclinable word that modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb (celeriter, “quickly”; iam, “already”). Latin adverbs do not inflect, though many form comparative and superlative degrees.

adverb.title: Adverb
adverb.html: An indeclinable word that modifies a verb, an adjective, or another adverb (celeriter, “quickly”; iam, “already”). Latin adverbs do not inflect, though many form comparative and superlative degrees.

interj.title: Interjection
interj.html: An invariant exclamatory word, used to express emotion or call attention (o!, “oh!”; ecce, “behold!”). Interjections stand outside the grammatical structure of the sentence.

interjection.title: Interjection
interjection.html: An invariant exclamatory word, used to express emotion or call attention (o!, “oh!”; ecce, “behold!”). Interjections stand outside the grammatical structure of the sentence.

enclitic.title: Enclitic
enclitic.html: A particle attached to the end of the preceding word rather than written separately (-que = “and”; -ne = question marker; -ve = “or”). Primaque in dictionary order is prima + -que.

num.title: Numeral
num.html: A word that denotes number — cardinal (unus, duo, tres; “one”, “two”, “three”), ordinal (primus, secundus; “first”, “second”), distributive (singuli, bini; “one each”, “two each”), or multiplicative (semel, bis; “once”, “twice”). Some are indeclinable; unus, duo, tres and the hundreds inflect.

numeral.title: Numeral
numeral.html: A word that denotes number — cardinal (unus, duo, tres; “one”, “two”, “three”), ordinal (primus, secundus; “first”, “second”), distributive (singuli, bini; “one each”, “two each”), or multiplicative (semel, bis; “once”, “twice”). Some are indeclinable; unus, duo, tres and the hundreds inflect.

nom.title: Nominative
nom.html: The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It's also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

nominative.title: Nominative
nominative.html: The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It's also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

gen.title: Genitive
gen.html: The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl's book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun's dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

genitive.title: Genitive
genitive.html: The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl's book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun's dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

dat.title: Dative
dat.html: The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

dative.title: Dative
dative.html: The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

acc.title: Accusative
acc.html: The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

accusative.title: Accusative
accusative.html: The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

abl.title: Ablative
abl.html: A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

ablative.title: Ablative
ablative.html: A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

voc.title: Vocative
voc.html: The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

vocative.title: Vocative
vocative.html: The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

sg.title: Singular
sg.html: A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

singular.title: Singular
singular.html: A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

pl.title: Plural
pl.html: A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

plural.title: Plural
plural.html: A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

masc.title: Masculine
masc.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that's just a fact about them; it doesn't always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

masculine.title: Masculine
masculine.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that's just a fact about them; it doesn't always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

fem.title: Feminine
fem.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

feminine.title: Feminine
feminine.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

neut.title: Neuter
neut.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

neuter.title: Neuter
neuter.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

1st-person.title: 1st person
1st-person.html: Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

2nd-person.title: 2nd person
2nd-person.html: Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3rd-person.title: 3rd person
3rd-person.html: Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There's no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

pres.title: Present
pres.html: The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

present.title: Present
present.html: The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

imperf.title: Imperfect
imperf.html: The past tense of ongoing or repeated action (amabat, “he was loving” / “he used to love”). Built on the present stem with -ba- infix.

imperfect.title: Imperfect
imperfect.html: The past tense of ongoing or repeated action (amabat, “he was loving” / “he used to love”). Built on the present stem with -ba- infix.

fut.title: Future
fut.html: The tense of action yet to happen (amabit, “he will love”). Different conjugations form the future with different endings.

future.title: Future
future.html: The tense of action yet to happen (amabit, “he will love”). Different conjugations form the future with different endings.

perf.title: Perfect
perf.html: The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

perfect.title: Perfect
perfect.html: The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

plup.title: Pluperfect
plup.html: The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

pluperfect.title: Pluperfect
pluperfect.html: The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

futperf.title: Future perfect
futperf.html: The tense of action that will have been completed by some future point (amaverit, “he will have loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -eri- infix.

future-perfect.title: Future perfect
future-perfect.html: The tense of action that will have been completed by some future point (amaverit, “he will have loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -eri- infix.

ind.title: Indicative
ind.html: The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

indicative.title: Indicative
indicative.html: The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

subj.title: Subjunctive
subj.html: The mood of possibility, wish, command in subordinate clauses, hypothetical condition, and other non-assertive uses (amet, “may he love” / “let him love”). One of the most flexible features of Latin.

subjunctive.title: Subjunctive
subjunctive.html: The mood of possibility, wish, command in subordinate clauses, hypothetical condition, and other non-assertive uses (amet, “may he love” / “let him love”). One of the most flexible features of Latin.

imp.title: Imperative
imp.html: The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

imperative.title: Imperative
imperative.html: The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

inf.title: Infinitive
inf.html: A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb's dictionary entry.

infinitive.title: Infinitive
infinitive.html: A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb's dictionary entry.

pap.title: Present active participle
pap.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing ongoing action by the subject (amans, “loving”). It declines as a third-declension adjective with one ending, agreeing with its noun in case, number, and gender, and is built on the present stem.

present-active-participle.title: Present active participle
present-active-participle.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing ongoing action by the subject (amans, “loving”). It declines as a third-declension adjective with one ending, agreeing with its noun in case, number, and gender, and is built on the present stem.

ppp.title: Perfect passive participle
ppp.html: The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb's passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

perfect-passive-participle.title: Perfect passive participle
perfect-passive-participle.html: The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb's passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

fap.title: Future active participle
fap.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing intended or impending action by the subject (amaturus, “about to love”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and combines with forms of esse to make the active periphrastic conjugation.

future-active-participle.title: Future active participle
future-active-participle.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing intended or impending action by the subject (amaturus, “about to love”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and combines with forms of esse to make the active periphrastic conjugation.

fpp.title: Future passive participle
fpp.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing necessity or impending action upon the subject — also called the gerundive (amandus, “about to be loved” / “to be loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and forms the passive periphrastic with esse.

future-passive-participle.title: Future passive participle
future-passive-participle.html: A non-finite verbal adjective expressing necessity or impending action upon the subject — also called the gerundive (amandus, “about to be loved” / “to be loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective and forms the passive periphrastic with esse.

act.title: Active
act.html: The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

active.title: Active
active.html: The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

pass.title: Passive
pass.html: The voice in which the subject undergoes the action (liber a puero legitur, “the book is read by the boy”). Latin has distinct passive endings for the present system; the perfect passive is built from the + a form of esse.

passive.title: Passive
passive.html: The voice in which the subject undergoes the action (liber a puero legitur, “the book is read by the boy”). Latin has distinct passive endings for the present system; the perfect passive is built from the + a form of esse.
